All right. Good afternoon, minister. Welcome along to the committee. Pleasure to have you here with us and your team. Uh, we have an hour and a half sit down for this afternoon's hearing on the appropriation for Vote Foreign Affairs. Uh, just before we kick into action on this, uh, we are live streaming.

I would like to acknowledge visiting delegation from the Solomon Islands. Just had the opportunity to meet them briefly before you've joined us. Minister. It's wonderful to have them over here looking at scrutiny week and action in our parliament this week. I'm sure they'll have some wonderful stories to take back to the Solemn Island. So, as I said, we have an hour and a half sit down.

Uh, you will have received a copy of the, uh, indicative agenda that we will, uh, use as a guide for this afternoon's session, uh, across a range of different areas. We welcome your introductory comments and then I'm sure there will be many questions from members as we traverse those areas over the course of the afternoon.

Over to you. Uh, well, Mr. Chairman, thank you. And again, uh, welcome to the, uh, guest from the Solomon Islands. It's, uh, great that they'll have a chance to hopefully see, uh, proper scrutiny of, uh, ministerial portfolio and the taxpayer expenditure.

In the interest of brevity, I thought I might just hand out my, uh, opening address rather than to read it to you, if that's agreeable. And then I'll introduce the team. I've got on the sections of consideration that are laid out in your agenda. Okay. Alright. Thank you. So, I'll give you a chance to have a look at that, and you tell me when you're ready to hear who the team is, who will be, uh, helping out in this, uh, committee hearing.

Very good. Thank you. Okay. Look, so I've got Tahar McPherson on my right. Um, the overview of appropriations. I've got Lucy Duncan on my left on Phase Diplomacy is your third section. We've got Benal Patis on International Development Corporation. We've got behind us again, Bernadette Kavanaugh on Antarctica, Victoria Hellman, the others helping out are Graham Morton and Paula Wilson here as well.

Excellent. Very good. Alright, we'll dive straight into questions. First question in the honorable opinion. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And, uh, thank you minister. Uh, first, uh, comment. Congratulations on a very busy travel schedule. Uh, and I think as I read through your introductory remarks here, if I may, uh, pick out, uh, the point you make about the Pacific.

Uh, and we note, uh, in the budget and acknowledge, uh, time limited funding around some of the economic, uh, sorry, the climate challenges and the security matters in the Pacific. Uh, I wondered if you would like to elaborate a little bit more with respect to, uh, the seen impact of that funding and why in the budget acknowledging it was time limited.

Uh, we haven't, uh, you haven't seen fit or the government hasn't seen fit, sorry, to continue to support those initiatives moving forward. Well, usually initiatives of that shape would've a time limit much more extended than that was, uh, the case when we inherited it.

Mm-hmm. So it had a run out period, two years too early. Uh, and so you can call any expression like fiscal cliff and things like that there, but the answer is we were caught by that, not for buses for and having to find money for it in a very serious, difficult time at, uh, whilst we were coval with that, doing a review of our expenditure.

And so, uh, the, um, position that was made very, very clear was that whilst the 2025 budget was there, it was, uh, up for review in 2026 and further consideration earlier than you would think. That's right. 'cause usually you'd step up and be saying 2025, it means 2028, but that wasn't the case.

So, uh, we are still focused on that. And, uh, looking at areas where, uh, resilience and structural, uh, objectives are probably more important. We are caught with the fact that we are in the middle of, uh, a change by the United States that represents huge sums of money, uh, which requires us to not, I'll say just accept the circumstance, but look alongside Australia and much wider for other countries to help out in the Pacific because we believe that maybe, and this is not a criticism of the past, but, uh, we may have not have looked as wide

as we possibly could for partners mm-hmm. To help out. They can only say no, but if you don't ask them, the answer's always gonna be no. So that's what we are trying to do, extending much wider than people think around the world. And some of the responses have been of genuine interest. And, uh, we hope to, uh, take that from the, uh, sort of, uh, early stages of consideration to something real.

So without giving the details, the prospects are better than the look on this current budgetary circumstance in our view. If I may supplementary Thank you, Mr. Chair. Does that lend itself to the ongoing Pacific Reset that I know you've spoken of in the past, and the view that the Pacific Reset is seen as a good, strong thing, uh, with respect to the way New Zealand works in the Pacific?

With your answer in mind, does that lend itself to tracking in the right direction for the reset that you've let in chance? Well, the reality is we have, uh, been involved in a reset since 2018. It's early in 2018, but, uh, here we are in reconsidering the circumstances, which even in that short place of time have of late dramatically changed.

Mm-hmm. And as a consequence, um, we have not finalized our considerations other than just say that we are looking as wide as we possibly can and how we might get a greater, uh, assistance to the, um, blue continent, which is 31% of the world's surface.

Uh, but with other partners who, uh, even in the, those restraint economic times we believe are willing to help out. Mm-hmm. Now, um, the, the reset is the concept. Yeah. Because it began a long time ago, in fact, about 20 years ago when some of us thought, hang on a minute, we're not paying enough attention to our own backyard.

Mm-hmm. Or in our case, our front yard. Mm-hmm. Right. So, getting to where we've got here from say, the early nineties, it's been, uh, an essential consideration. We just need, though, to have a better understanding in our political system that New Zealand should be doing a whole lot more.

Mm-hmm. Uh, and seeking it to achieve what was only achieved once in, uh, the year 70, uh, two to 75 under Norman Kirk, which was upsetting at about 40, uh, 0.54%. And we've got up to 0.35 in the past. And, uh, here we are starting again to at least set out to get a return to that.

Again, I wanna emphasize that I think we've got other people who have prepared other countries prepared to help where we should have engaged 'em earlier. Very good. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thank you Minister, um, for your time today. Uh, just noting your comments and your opening remarks in regards to New Zealand's response to the Israel Hamas, uh, conflict and New Zealand's response in terms of upholding international law, including international humanitarian law.

And I did want to first acknowledge, um, the sound decision that you made, minister, in terms of imposing those recent travel bans. Um, I wonder in terms of the forward looking approach, given the escalating conflict we are seeing, but also the really concerning restrictions around aid, whether there's an opportunity within the budget lines for New Zealand to reconsider joining South Africa's case at the ICJ.

Um, just noting the multi-category budget line, um, on page 97 of the appropriations does have that additional allocation for advocacy. So I'm wondering whether that might be the place that it would sit. Well, it depends on how you are interpreting legally the South African case and its current adjudication.

The number of claims are being made about it, which aren't true. That's to its international finality, if you grasp what I'm saying. And in terms of the Decision you raised, that's, I'm asking you In terms of the decision being able to constitute international law. I think there, there could be some value in New Zealand, uh, contributing some views in terms of whether the case for genocide is made up, But it's not constituting international law at this point in time, is it?

Well, if you've got a different view, I'd like to hear it because the troubles, the trouble with too much in foreign affairs, sad to say, is that people tell you what they want to happen rather than what is happening. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah. Our job is to find out what is happening and be ultra cautiously in, we're a small country long, all long away from these circumstances being asked to make all sorts of, uh, contributions when, as you know, in our neighborhood, we've got huge demands.

We've got, and our own domestic population got huge demands. And so getting the um, uh, understanding correct is critical. Can I document Thank you minister. And perhaps, um, I could also ask another question in relation to our efforts internationally.

I noted the decision for New Zealand to attend the upcoming international conference on the two two state solution in New York, which I think is, is an extremely positive step for us to take. Um, does that indicate that there is recognition that, uh, or there's an understanding that recognizing Palestine now could be a useful tool for peace if that were to be done early?

Look, uh, across the political divide, this country has agreed in supporting the two state solution. So that's where we've got unity on. The question you ask here is the difficulty. This would be an acceptance of a state of affairs that does not exist.

I've spoken to the Palestinian authority, I've spoken to the Egyptians, I've spoken to Emirates, to Jakarta, and I've spoken to a lot of players in this. And so my colleagues and so my, and so foreign affairs, this would be setting a unique standard way below that which we've always set.

And once having changed our standard, the call on that for a lesser standard will be of f of a frequency that we need to understand now. And I say now whether we can accept that now and rather than just make this a unique case, 'cause it will have ramifications. We have always said, it's not a question of that, it's when, but how do we establish who is to be that authority?

It's generally difficult question. Mm-hmm. Steve Abel. Yeah. Thank you. Just follow following on from that. Um, even outside the question of recognizing the state of the state of the Palestinians, recognizing the right of the Palestinians that the people to exist, I suppose is the challenge. Um, and the ICJs advisory opinion on the 24th of July at International Court of Justice declared that the prolonged presence of Israel and the whole of the Palestinian territory occupied since 1967, including the culinary regime, was unlawful,

aimed at annexation and undermined the integrity of the Palestinian people in the occupied Palestinian Territories and sought to acquire sovereignty over an occupied territory. And I guess the challenge is, you know, the special rapport Rapporteur for Palestine, um, says that she suggests that genocide should be seen as an integral and instrumental in the aim of full Israeli colonization of Palestinian land while removing as many Palestinians as possible.

Is, is there not a problem, minister, if we can't acknowledge the intent of Israel to annexation, colonization and genocide in this instance, does that not affect our ability to trust, for example, that Israel has genuine intent to feed the Palestinians?

Because it would seem to me that they go to get food while starving and it's a shooting galleries that would be more consistent with an intent to genocide, which is what seems to be obviously happening. Are you answering question or you asking is number one. Number two, the critical words you used at the start of that was the advisory opinion.

Mm-hmm. Those words are critical because it puts in context the primary question I got from the first question, 'cause it's an advisory opinion, is not of the status that you're asserting. Sure. But do you have an opinion on it, minister? Well, well, we've all got opinions, but they have no good to anyone unless they've got value Would, That's the restraint we should, we've all got as a, because we don't, talking about Winston Peters or Mr.

Abel here, we're talking about a country called New Zealand. That's right. Reputation. That's Right. And so in terms of New Zealand's reputation, where we have clearly, um, in our better moments stood against, uh, clear breaches and atrocities and acts against the rule of international law and acts against human rights, are you comfortable that we are doing sufficient in that space when really it seems that we are caught up with a desire to remain on side with an older ally like the United States, whilst they themselves are complicit in supporting

Israel's atrocities? Well, Again, this is not a question, this is a statement you're making by the way of the guise of a question. Well, I'm Trying to ask, It's not for me. I'm not, I'm just giving you my view, so the chairman to decide that. But I'm entitled to judge what I'm being, uh, whether I'm being asked something or told someone. Do you perceive it as a genocide, Mr. Minister? Well, again, you know, it's not my perception that matters.

It's what the respected international, uh, legal opinion would be. And that has not been arrived at in the way it was framed. Okay. Alright. Tim Costley. Oh, thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thanks minister for your time. I, I'd like to reflect on some of your opening, uh, remarks where you talk about the new mini lateral groupings and, and hop on perhaps my old hobby horse of the IP four, but New Zealand's relationship with Australia, uh, Japan, South Korea, and that as a means of engaging with nato.

I know the Prime Minister's heading there next week. Um, as part of that IP four meeting, I'd be interested, given your ex extensive, um, uh, work overseas over the last year to hear your reflections on, on that and which other, maybe mini lateral, as you described them, uh, groupings, uh, of particular interest to New Zealand. And secondly, uh, not just them as a means for us to engage with a big organization like nato, but what that can mean and challenges closer to home, whether that's South China Sea, uh, geostrategic competition in the Pacific and, and,

and how that, that helps us. Well, you've heard the expression from small acorns, great oak trees graph, but, uh, a min later organizational structure finally led to the comprehensive progressive TPPA. That's what it finally led to. But New Zealand was a very significant initiator of that process into something far, far bigger, and now proceed by some to being, uh, the, you know, principle potential, um, economic cooperation arrangement in the absence of other things at this present time.

So, uh, we are just saying that we are looking everywhere we can possibly. And, uh, a country like New Zealand is as driven as it always was since a certain term voter book, a book about the instability of a dependent economy. And we're as unstable in that context as ever were. And we've gotta work a whole lot harder than we ever have to ensure that we survive in this world, um, because no one answers a living.

Uh, we've gotta get out there and market and sell ourselves as hard as we can go. And it's starting to work. And, and I wonder Minister Mitch, thank you. Uh, um, I, I I think most Kiwis would understand that we have longstanding relationships through our sort of five eye partner countries, maybe even FPDA, but, but I'm interested to see where, where, uh, you or the ministry, um, through, through you are, are focusing.

Is it, are, are there other organizations like the IP four? Is there other, uh, entities through Southeast Asia where perhaps there hasn't been, uh, emphasis in the past, but which presents opportunity in the future? Yes. Um, answer that is where we're looking at every opportunity we possibly can, but certain constraints and that those constraints are resources.

But I think we've imaged that, uh, over recent times. All right. Any here today? Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thank you again, minister, going by your comments and your introductory remarks there. And the fact that as I examined the vote, uh, for foreign affairs, there seems to be offsets of some money's gone from here.

Now it's put to the focus on there. Are you satisfied then that, uh, the budget as it's set out for Vote Foreign Affairs will, uh, achieve the desired outcomes that you've set for respect to, uh, our foreign policy and the role of foreign affairs, uh, from here into the coming year?

Any minister foreign affairs is satisfied with their, they not just not fit for the job. That's A fact. Right? We just gotta get our country to understand that there's modeling out there like, uh, Singapore, like Ireland, like Croatia, modern Croatia, and the full of communism there.

But it's dramatically, dramatically shown that the investment in foreign affairs, trade utilities and staff and capacity is a payoff in a big way. That's been very hard to sell inside New Zealand. We are just not learning the lessons of others or in Singapore's case, but we used to help under the Colombo plan go straight past us with rational same economic policies and investments that have had a huge return for them.

Mm-hmm. Thank you for that, Dan. Kira, Thank you. Thank you, minister. Um, I acknowledge the e extensive travel and work that you've done, um, in the past 18 months to deliver a good foreign affairs reputation for New Zealand. I'm interested to know your views on, uh, how that extensive work has impacted the reputation of New Zealand as a global partner.

Um, that's an invitation to a modesty. I mean, the world's gotta give you a comment on that, but, uh, real, our real point is that in so many areas, uh, restraint or economy or um, modesty of engagement, it's not been good for us. We just gotta go the extra mile and spend the extra time and also understand how rapidly the world's changing.

And you can do it by Zoom and you can do it by email, you can do it by phone. So there's nothing like the old Pacific way face-to-face. And, um, there's some of these, um, uh, uh, the, the lessons we've learned from that have been enormously engaging. And you, you're talking to somebody from Saudi Arabia's never seen anybody and asking them to invest in your country, and they think, uh, why?

And the answer is, well take the racing industry and we've got less than 8% of the horses in Australia, and we running almost 22%, 22% of the group won racists. That prick up the interest at real big time. Yeah. And when you say to em, I be the Melbourne Cup count times, and I seen you guys, you've never won the Melbourne Cup once you, you bought now horses, maybe you start winning.

And there they're interested already said them in its bad culture here, we've gotta market ourselves more. That interest is also in the em, in in the Emirates as well. So it goes in a lot of areas. And then I suppose one shock to be in America one day, seeing a person that used to be fronting, uh, the America's most wanted and he's selling of all things, something with a big name green lip mussel from New Zealand.

And wonder why on earth is my country not doing a whole lot more there? 'cause they've got anti cancerous properties. That's why they're selling it. We're building the biggest muscle power in the world, but where's our marketing? So, you know, it's a total operation of yield habits to come through your electorate, doesn't it? Or very It does indeed. That's right. Indeed. Well, I can see why you're taking interest.

I mean, but that's what we've gotta do. And all of a sudden you'll start seeing, um, that the trade ambitions you've got are starting to be met. We are just not doing enough. Thank you. Questions still under the first section? Or did, did you wanna move on?

Where Is aid Sort of transitioning between the third mine's on aid? Oh, yeah. Okay. Well, we'll, we'll wrap up the first section, then we'll go onto the trading diplomacy piece, and then we will you on and the IDC piece, if you're happy with that. Um, so under the trading diplomacy, first question. I Thank you, Mr. Chair.

Um, minister, there's been much talk about, uh, the doubling of export, uh, for particular focus on India, no prime minister's trade delegation, uh, and the not too distant past. Are you satisfied that the vote, uh, and money allocation will bring about any material?

Uh, uh, you call it material outcome? Uh, with respect to India, given, from what I can see, it looks like there has been no investment in, uh, direct, uh, increase in diplomatic capacity there in India. From what I could see at least, Uh, well before Mr.

Perles, uh, uh, has answered the question for you, Carl could just say this, that, am I satisfied with the vote? Well, I, the primary question said, no, I'm not. Uh, 'cause we need to do far more, uh, allow the Ian Singapore, uh, um, Celtic tiger example of what happens when you put more effort into it.

But that said, uh, it, the world's just dramatically changed in front of us, and, uh, all of a sudden our interest or in India is noticed. This is no nonsense. But, uh, went personally, I didn't go my first trip I went to, uh, for a number of reasons.

Um, but, uh, at that time we spoke to others you've never in the past made contact with, but because of the change circumstances, they were interested in spending far more time with us than before. But perhaps, uh, we couldn't answer the question. Uh, yeah. So just on India specifically, um, I, I was there last week for the, uh, for the negotiations and, um, there's been a, a real ramping up of the, um, of the work with India.

So we've had a, a full first round. We've had a number of intercessions, um, by video conference, uh, more than 400 meetings now with the Indians in the various chapter parts of the negotiation. So the negotiation is really in two parts. There's the disciplines and rules of the agreement, and there's the market access piece, and we've focused in on the, the 19 chapters that we have to negotiate.

And then, uh, at the same time, in parallel, we're focused on the market access commitments, which are not just in goods, but also in services. Uh, which is a, another rising, um, area of interest for us with the, um, with the Indian colleagues. I think one of the, one of the observations to make is the minister said is that, um, India has become a central player now, including in the trade area.

Everyone is wanting to negotiate, uh, with India until relatively recently, uh, they had not done, uh, many free trade agreements, but over the last year, uh, well, actually over the last three years, they've really ramped up. So they've finished a negotiation with Australia. Uh, they finished a negotiation with the United Arab Emirates. They finished a negotiation with the Floc, Norway, Switzerland, Iceland, uh, and Liechtenstein in Switzerland.

Uh, and they are in negotiations with, uh, a number of our key competitors, Chile, Peru, uh, but they are also crucially in negotiations with the European Union and the United States. And they've also finished a, an agreement recently with the, with the United Kingdom, they've announced substantive conclusion of negotiations.

So this is a, this is a really important negotiation for us, and that we need to, uh, kind of make progress on. It's not just in the trade area, of course, it's that wider relationship that we need to build that you would've seen, uh, during the Prime Minister's visit there. But in terms of our, uh, focus, I mean, the big challenge with India is, um, it is a, it has very high levels of protection.

So to give you some examples, um, in dairy, the protection range from 30 to 60%. And to make the observation that actually in none of the free trade agreements that India has negotiated, have they ever, uh, given any access on dairy? So not with the British, not with the Australians, and certainly not with Switzerland.

Norway and the colleagues from on sheet meat. Um, there's a 30% tariff. Uh, they did give, uh, Australia tariff free access on sheet meat, and we've seen a real shift. Uh, we've, our trade has declined, uh, and Australia's has nearly doubled in sheet meat alone, admittedly off a low base.

But nevertheless, the, the facts speak for themselves. Horticulture, the average tariff on horticultural products ranging from kiwi fruit through to apples, is between 50 and 60%. So these are colossal barriers. Um, and again, to take apples, uh, the Indians have never given anyone access, uh, in apples, not to the Australians, not to the British.

And they're determined that they won't give access to, uh, other partners there either. Seafood, the tariffs 30 to 50%, uh, that is a high, it's a high value market for New Zealand, but we are tiny in there. And the tariff is a significant barrier. And the Australians do have improved access, uh, there as well. So if you look across, uh, honey's, another good example, um, it's a, again, a really high value market, particularly for the manga, uh, honey, it's a 60% tariff there.

And again, India has never given access to anyone. Not the British, not the Australians, uh, not the after colleagues, not the UAE. So for all of these reasons, this is going to be a very difficult, uh, and challenging negotiation to look to advance. Um, but we have, uh, really focused in on this. So we have another round coming up, uh, in July, and I expect every month we will be in a rolling negotiation process with, uh, with India.

But it's really important to see India just as one part of the strategy. Uh, so there's the Ft A with India, but there's also the work that we've done, um, with the United Arab Emirates, which the government concluded at Pace nearly broke my team. Um, but that was done within three months. Uh, so, and then we also concluded the agreement with the GC, so Saudi Arabia, Arabia, and the other colleagues as well.

Those two agreements, if we can get them entered into force as soon as possible, that adds another 2.6 billion to our exports, uh, into the region just for those two alone. So this is an important part of the building of resilience strategy that we've got now, uh, in place. And I'm happy to talk a little bit about that strategy in more detail. But just on the India, uh, and the FDA piece, those are really big priorities for us at the moment.

Thank you. Yeah. Thank You, Mr. Chair. Um, just to start question on that. Um, given the challenges with these, the market with India, do you have a rough timeline of how long you expect this negotiation to take? Um, or what you'd like it to take? I guess?

So the, these, these things are difficult and no negotiator is ever gonna put a deadline on it, although I noticed that the Indians give us A rough idea what you like. That's why I rephrase here. Yes, minister? Yeah. Yes, Minister. Good answer. Um, so I mean, maybe to make one observation is that the Indians put out a press release at the end of our first, uh, negotiating round, saying they would like to finish the agreement this year.

Okay. Um, I think there's an important balance to be struck between equality of what you get. Mm-hmm. Uh, what is it that's reasonable to secure in the timeframe that you've got. Mm-hmm. And what kind of builtin agenda then you prosecute, uh, as part of your forward, uh, program with, uh, with those colleagues? Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Steve Abel, Thank you. Um, I've got a couple of questions, please minister.

How important do you think is our reputation for producing a high value natural food product? In terms of our export markets? Um, that's been our intense since the first, uh, frozen cargo meat left New Zealand in the 1880s To sell ourselves as the producer of the wine products.

So, um, are you aware of the very large growing non GMO food market globally, particularly in India and China, and the potential there for providing a, um, high value non-genetically modified food product? I'm very much aware of the claims as to the substance.

They don't quite equate. Do you think there's a risk if the government refuses the advice of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and trade, that we make trade and market access risk, uh, an important component of the Genetech bill currently, uh, before the select committee?

Uh, well, it's currently before the select committee, uh, now told us to take in public and submissions from all sorts of groups and, and to make sure the very process that happens at the select committee such as this process here is transparent and honest and that people are paid into.

So we're waiting for the outcome. So you think that the select committee should consider the advice of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, that the trade market access risk is a concern with us. They Wouldn't be appropriate for the minister To comment on influencing select committee Processes? Mr. Yes, I'd like to be in control of the committee. Very good. Next question, Walter.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, in terms of our relationship with China Minister, um, I wanted to ask whether you are happy that there is sufficient investment for us to, um, develop that relationship, um, in the coming years? Just very aware that, uh, in some years past, we haven't, and there have been quite restricted ministerial visits to China.

Um, one part of it. And then of course the Prime Minister is on his way to China. And the scope of that, uh, that visit, as far as I understand, is fairly a narrow focus on trade. And there has been some suggestion that there would be benefit in a broader agenda being discussed. Um, whether you have any thoughts?

Well, yes, I have a lot of thoughts on that. First thing that I would really ask people to consider is to try and think like the Chinese, because a lot of people don't, don't realize how long range they are in their planning and their thinking. They've never read Chairman Mao's books or we've read anything about China.

And I kind of think that you don't survive down all those thousands of years, uh, because you're not a clever people. So that's the first thing they get my full respect. The fact that some of us left there 5,000 years ago will turn this room at least it's neither here nor there. We never just owned up that. And even though some thought it was a comedy when I first said it, 9 9 6 9 5, they laughed at me not laughing.

Now, because the DNA is out there now, and it's con conclusively proven. But the other thing is that, uh, you know, good relationships are based on respectful differences of opinion. And I can ate properly submit the answer to my question by reciting the words of the Chinese ambassador just the other day in the last 48 hours, saying how excellent our relationship is.

I've got a question for you, minister, in relation to the extensive travel you've undertaken over for the last 18 months. Really keen to get some insights on the feedback you've received and the Pacific in particular from that. And then how, whether you anticipate maintaining that level of engagement over the coming 18 months as well.

I could tell you one that was rather amazing. We went up to, uh, Micronesia and two of the three asked us, uh, what's going wrong? And we said, what do you mean? They said, well, we've never seen you before. Yeah, we've been to the Marshals, but not the two. And that I was actually, uh, rather alarm because we are part of the Pacific Islands Forum.

And what we sort of do since then to use our utilities the cheapest way possible to assist them to be part and parcel of the, uh, Longwood engagement. And I think that's seriously important. Um, we have a view that the Pacific Islands Forum Forum is, uh, critical to our foreign policy is critical to, to a specific policy.

Its centrality is gotta be heightened and its work's gotta be improved dramatically. And we've gotta be play a serious role in that. Um, some of us can reflect on how in the past there was a Caity about the whole approach. I regret that it is seriously important now for us to understand our role.

And, um, the great thing about New Zealand is that we do understand the Pacific as good as any other country, uh, because of our connections in our long experience there. Um, the second thing about New Zealand is we've always regarded those countries regardless of their size as being our equals in the same way as we expect larger countries to treat us in the same way.

They're starting to have purpose now, but in the end they've got huge problems which they need assistance with. And you have to say, well, some option doesn't happen happen here from like minor countries. Where will they go? And how attempting it will be to do, to resource themselves from somewhere else, perhaps with not the same intent and interest as we have.

Mm-hmm. There are major challenges there and it's not gonna be easy, but, uh, I kind of think that, um, and that's why we've had these cross-party tours when we've gone there to try and understand, ensure that Parliament understands our critical role when it comes to Pacific Islands Forum. Just before we finish up on the trade and diplomacy section, I'm really keen to get some insight into the progress on non tariff barriers.

It's a piece of work this committee's had a particular interest in over the course of this parliament, and we appreciate there's been some very, uh, good progress in resolving some of those. Uh, but there remain ongoing challenges there, and in the context of looking to double the value of exports, very keen to get an update on the progress being made there.

Yeah. So, um, sorry. Yes. Um, so on on non-tariff barriers. So this, this is a very broad area as you know from the, um, briefing. We provided the, the committee, uh, maybe start with a classical version of it, um, that is the barrier at the border. Um, and those non tariff barriers, we've got a watch list, 194 items are on that watch list of which, um, 125, uh, in the food and, uh, beverage sector plus, uh, the primary industry.

So those two alone account for the overwhelming bulk of the, the challenge we face internationally. And we know that the value of these, um, varies from, uh, we've got TBS on our list, uh, that are worth about five to 6 million. And we've got TBS on our list with some of our big partners that are in excess of 20 billions in terms of, uh, the amount of trade that would be unleashed if you could get rid of that barrier.

So the classical non tariff barrier is the one that you have, um, at the border, uh, and tends to be certification requirements. It can be a packaging requirement, uh, big dealing recently with a case where the packaging requirement changed three times in a year. That's clearly designed to try to keep you out.

Uh, so those are the kinds of things we look to, to challenge. Uh, I wanna speak about how we do that in a, in a second, but just to give you some practical examples of that. Then there are the standards. So those are the classical sort of non tariff barriers that we look at in terms of what they are at the border. Uh, then we have the barriers that are within the border, so, uh, domestic regulatory settings, but also, uh, agricultural subsidies.

Uh, so those kinds of things that have a really, uh, very significant effect, for example, on prices. So to take subsidies, um, in the agriculture space alone, uh, we know that just four economies account for 80% of all global subsidies that are determined to be price distorting.

And we know that lowers, uh, global prices for us, for New Zealand farmers, uh, by up to 14%. So it is a really significant, uh, non tariff barrier that we have to grapple with. Decans did some really interesting research recently showing that if a 50% cut in EU support for cheese production, that would translate into an 8% increase in the global price and therefore return back into, uh, New Zealand agricultural exporters.

Hmm. So that's another form of those barriers. Now, there are multiple ways in which we look to deal with, um, with these barriers. And the first is of course, very directly. Um, and so our embassies offshore, uh, at various points of escalation right through until the ambassador, you kind of go in second secretary level, deputy head of mission level in the ambassador level bilaterally, trying to find a, a resolution to, uh, to that.

And I know the minister's been directly involved recently in, uh, one of the challenges we've had on Halal with, uh, with, uh, Indonesia. So a longstanding challenge, uh, there. So those sort of set of escalation points bilaterally. Uh, that's one mechanism. The second mechanisms to work as a group. Uh, so a group of, uh, WTO member states, um, we might work together in the capital.

So capital X, a group of, uh, embassies will get together and say, look, we share the same problem. We need to knock down this barrier, and there'll be a lobbying effort, uh, that, that we bring together there. Then you've got a sort of a further escalation point where you would say, right, do we have a free trade agreement with that partner? Now, it's really important to remember that one of the critical pieces of our job is not just the negotiation of the free trade, but it's the implementation and the enforcement of the agreement.

And that implementation and enforcement is a really important part of the work that the ministry does. And, uh, particularly in my group, but not only in my group. Also in terms of what we, uh, do offshore. So all of the agreements that we have have something called the goods committee. Many of them also have an additional, uh, working group on non tariff barriers.

That is a formal legal mechanism within this international treaty that we've negotiated that allows us to raise and obliges our counterpart to respond to a problem that we're experiencing at the border. We are very active users of those mechanisms. So one of the benefits that we see from these agreements is that big partners, we go in at the embassy level, uh, nothing really happens, but we then use this legal architecture that we have.

And in many of our agreements, for example, Ian, Australia and New Zealand Free Trade Agreement and CBTP, there are obligations within, in the case of CBTP, within 30 days in the case of the Ian Australia, New Zealand FDA, within 45 days to respond in writing to your query. And then there's an additional step, which is then used, which is to meet, uh, and that can be virtually or in person in a committee structure with all of our other colleagues, whether it's in CPTP or whether it's in, in, again, to tackle those non tariff barriers.

Now, what we find is that, uh, that sort of peer, uh, kind of review process, when people have to appear in front of a committee to explain these barriers, that often has a, what we call the Dracula effect. Sunlight is shone on it, and we find that the, uh, the non tariff barrier starts to wither. So the free trade agreements are there.

Uh, the other mechanism we have is the SPS and the technical barriers for trade committee at the World Trade Organization. We're one of the highest users of that mechanism. We're in the top 3% of using, uh, those committees to ask questions of other countries. And we do that without fear or favor, whoever it is, whether it's Indonesia, whether it's European Union, uh, whether it's Japan.

Uh, we've raised, uh, our concerns about these non tariff barriers that are there. And sort of in terms of our sort of next steps that we're doing, one of the things that, um, we are now doing is we are gonna create a new unit, uh, because of the, the pernicious nature of all of these non-trads. We're creating a new unit within, uh, the trade economic group within the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade to really step up our work in support of, um, uh, business here.

So the implementation piece is one part. There's also legal enforcement, uh, and that is another really important dimension that we're now increasingly reaching into the toolbox for, uh, when we find that people are not, um, uh, delivering or, or, or we think they're actually cheating us in terms of their legal obligations to us through these treaties.

A good example of that is, uh, our friends from Canada, uh, where we have taken a dispute against them, not through the WTO, but through the C-P-T-V-P, uh, and we are now working through. So we won that case. The panel found in our favor, there's been an appeal process, uh, and now we're in the process of working with candidates to ensure that it fully implements the agreement.

And as a G seven economy, of course, we have no doubt that they will, um, fully implement, um, the findings of the panel. Uh, and I expect a resolution to, to happen, uh, reasonably swiftly. The amount of trade that's at stake there is between 45 and 120 million, uh, 16 tariff rate quotas that the Canadians have used and now view, and in the view of the panel, uh, abused as a way to restrict our, our access.

So again, another non tariff barrier, but another of those mechanisms that we've got in our trade agreements to try to, uh, change that.
